lesson.menu_desc: Reading reports for data: gap-fill, word banks & the language of certainty -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- Reading the Numbers
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Helen Mendes | Aula 4 -- Reading the Numbers

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: the final result of an action or event
a short, general description of something
a number that represents an amount or value
an early version of a document before it is final
an important point or stage in a project
a general direction in which something is changing
a short statement of the main points
comments about how good or useful something is

reading.rtitle: Reading the Numbers: How Teams Turn Data into Decisions
paras: Every week, millions of professionals open a report full of numbers. A good report does not just list figures; it tells a story. The first page usually gives a short overview, and the last page offers a summary of the main points. In between, the data shows what is really happening.

The most useful skill is spotting the trend. One number on its own means very little. But when you compare this quarter with the last one, a pattern appears. Is the line going up or down? Did the team reach an important milestone, or miss it? These questions turn raw data into a clear outcome.

Finally, smart readers pay attention to language. Words like 'clearly show' and 'prove' sound certain, while 'suggests' and 'seems to' sound careful. Good professionals choose words that match the data -- and they always ask for feedback before they share the final draft.
reading.source: Adapted from a business-skills guide, The Working Reader

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the article?
choices: A good report should list as many figures as possible on every page.
Reading reports well means finding the story and the trend in the data, not just the numbers.
Professionals should always sound completely certain when they present data.

items: A good report tells a story, not just a list of figures.
The text says a good report does not just list figures; it tells a story.
One number on its own usually shows a clear trend.
The text says one number alone means little -- you have to compare quarters.
Words like 'suggests' and 'seems to' sound careful, not fully certain.
The text contrasts them with the certain words 'clearly show' and 'prove'.
Smart readers share the final draft before they ask for any feedback.
They always ask for feedback before they share the final draft.

gapfill.text: Notice: a figure is one number, but we often talk about the figures (all the numbers) in a report. An overview is short and general at the start; a summary repeats the main points at the end.

lf.text: Notice: strong words like 'clearly show' and 'prove' sound very certain -- use them only with solid data. When you are not sure, soften the claim with 'suggests', 'seems to', or 'may'. It sounds more careful and more professional.

bank.label: Useful language for reporting
items: As you can see in the chart...
Let me give you a quick overview.
You present this quarter's sales figures to your team. Give a short overview, then describe the main trend.
A colleague sends you a draft report. Give feedback: one thing that is clear, and one thing to improve.
Your project reached an important milestone. Summarize the outcome for your manager in three sentences.
The data is not certain yet. Report a possible trend, but soften your claim with 'seems to', 'suggests', or 'may'.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = figures, 2 = overview, 3 = trend, 4 = milestone, 5 = feedback.
Model overview: 'Here is a quick overview of the quarter. The sales figures are strong and the trend is clearly upward.'
Model softened claim: 'The data suggests a small rise; it seems to point to steady growth.'
Model feedback: 'The summary is clear. You might want to add the final outcome and the client feedback.'
answerkey.note: Strong claims ('clearly show', 'prove') need solid data; soften with 'suggests', 'seems to', or 'may' when you are not fully sure.

listenings.text: Good morning, everyone. Here is a quick overview of the quarter. Our sales figures rose by twelve percent, and the trend is clearly upward in every region. We reached our main milestone two weeks early. The early feedback from clients has been very positive, so the overall outcome is strong. Well done, team.

Hi, it is Daniel from finance. I just read the first draft of your report. The summary is clear, but a few figures on page three do not match the data. Could you check them before the meeting? Once the numbers are right, I think the final outcome will look great. Thanks, talk soon.

extra_audio.text: Helen opens the draft of the quarterly report. She reads the overview and the key figures. The figures show a clear upward trend. The team celebrates an important milestone. Helen sends her feedback on the final outcome.
